Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd kind of just study whenever I felt like it or when something was due soon. Most weeks I'd put in maybe 17 hours, but it was scattered all over the place - sometimes late at night, sometimes in random bursts during the day. I'm more of a hands-on learner anyway, so sitting down to organize a schedule never really clicked for me.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. I'd go days without touching anything academic, then suddenly realize something was coming up and try to cram. Deadlines were pretty much the only thing that got me moving. Regular study times just felt too rigid for how I operate.
Q3: Usually I logged in because I got a notification about something or I was bored and figured I should at least check what was happening. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if there was anything new, scroll around a bit, maybe watch part of a video. It wasn't really about being motivated to learn - more like checking in to make sure I wasn't completely lost.
Q4: Yeah, for a while there I was logging in pretty regularly, almost daily at some points. I think it became routine because I'd just open it up while doing other stuff online. It wasn't deep engagement or anything, but I'd browse around, check discussions, see what other people were posting. It was more of a habit than actual studying.
Q5: The videos were probably the most useful for me because I could watch them while doing other things or rewind parts I didn't get. Discussion posts helped too because seeing how other students explained things sometimes made more sense than the official materials. I learn better when I can see something in action rather than just reading about it.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the readings and the longer written assignments. Reading dense academic stuff just doesn't stick for me - I need to do something or see it demonstrated. The assignments I kept putting off because they felt overwhelming and I didn't really know where to start with them. My completion rate on those was pretty bad.
Q7: If I could see how something would actually help me pass or understand a concept quickly, I'd do it. Anything that felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to the exam, I'd skip. I needed to feel like my time was actually getting me somewhere, not just checking boxes.
Q8: Optional stuff basically didn't exist for me. If it wasn't required, I probably wasn't doing it unless I was genuinely curious about the topic. Required activities at least had some pressure behind them, even if I still procrastinated on those too. Without a deadline or consequence, I just couldn't make myself care enough.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download videos or PDFs and then study them on my phone when I was out or didn't have good internet. I also talked through concepts with mates or looked stuff up on YouTube when the course materials weren't making sense. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS.
Q10: There were times I'd be working through problems on paper or rewatching downloaded videos multiple times, but none of that would show up anywhere. I'd also spend time just thinking through concepts while doing other stuff - like at work or commuting. That kind of processing doesn't leave a digital footprint.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd open a video once, download it or take screenshots of the important bits, and then never go back to the LMS version. Same with some of the lecture slides - grabbed them once and studied from my phone or laptop offline. Made more sense than using up data or dealing with slow loading.
Q12: Around midterms I just hit a wall. I had work stuff going on, limited money causing stress, and the assignments were piling up. It felt like I was already too far behind to catch up properly, so I kind of mentally checked out for a bit. The CBT sessions helped me get my head back together somewhat.
Q13: Definitely. When I was stressed about money or tired from work, the last thing I wanted to do was log into the LMS and deal with coursework. Confusion was a big one too - if I didn't understand something, I'd just close the tab rather than struggle through it. Easier to avoid than to face it.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have videos playing but I'd be on my phone or thinking about something else entirely. Or I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. My mind just wasn't in it even though technically I was 'engaged' with the platform.
Q15: The assignments were the main thing I avoided. I knew they were important but the low scores I kept getting made me feel like why bother. It's hard to motivate yourself when you feel like you're going to fail anyway. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd say the same thing.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only reason I did anything. I'd cram right before something was due, which is probably why my assignment scores were so low - I wasn't giving myself enough time to do them properly. Without a deadline, I just drifted along.
Q17: When I got feedback, if it was negative, it kind of made me want to engage less. Like, what's the point if I'm just going to do badly anyway? Positive feedback was rare enough that when it happened, it did give me a small boost. But mostly feedback just reminded me I wasn't doing well.
Q18: The course having so many different modules and resources actually made it harder for me. I'd get overwhelmed by all the options and not know what to prioritize. If it had been more straightforward - like, do this, then this, then this - I might have kept up better. The flexibility kind of worked against me.
Q19: The sheer number of online courses I was juggling made everything harder. Having 18 courses meant nothing got my full attention. The LMS itself was fine, but the course design assumed I had more time and focus than I actually did. Shorter, more focused modules would have helped.
Q20: That's basically me, isn't it? I was logging in heaps, watching videos, checking discussions, but my actual submitted work was terrible. I think it's because being present online is easy - it's passive. Actually doing assignments requires energy and focus I didn't always have. You can look busy without being productive.
Q21: I kind of get that too. My exam score was decent even though my assignment completion was awful. Some people just learn differently or study outside the system. You can understand material without constantly logging in - especially if you're using other resources or studying in ways that don't get tracked.
Q22: Logs can show when you're online and what you click on, but they can't show if you're actually paying attention or understanding anything. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with classmates, the thinking you do away from the screen. It's like measuring how long someone sits at a desk versus how much work they actually did.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in effort to understand it. It's not just about showing up or clicking through materials. I'd say I was engaged in some ways - I was curious about certain topics - but my motivation was so low that it didn't translate into consistent effort or good work.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I was dealing with financial stress and working to make ends meet while trying to study. It wouldn't show the mental effort it took just to log in some days, or the times I was genuinely trying to understand something but struggling. It also wouldn't show that I actually did okay on exams despite everything, which means something was getting through even when my online activity looked scattered.
